coredata.dc:title: Chemically derived graphene quantum dots for high-strain sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shujun
Lenzini, Francesco
Chen, Dechao
Tanner, Philip
Han, Jisheng
Thiel, David
Lobino, Mirko
Li, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) refer to graphene fragments with a lateral dimension typically less than 100 nm, which possess unique electrical and optical properties due to the quantum confinement effect. In this study, we demonstrate that chemically derived graphene quantum dots show great potential for making highly stretchable and cost-effective strain sensors via an electron tunneling mechanism. Stretchable strain sensors are critical devices for the field of flexible or wearable electronics which are expected to maintain function up to high strain values (> 30%). However, strain sensors based on conventional materials (i.e. metal or semiconductors) or metal nanoparticles (e.g. gold or silver nanoparticles) only work within a small range of strain (i.e. the former have a working range < 1% and the latter < 3%). In this study, by simply dropcasting solution-processed GQDs between the interdigitated electrodes on polydimethylsiloxane, we obtained devices that can function in the range from 0.06% to over 50% tensile strain with both the sensitivity and working range conveniently adjustable by the concentration of GQDs applied. This study provides a new concept for practical applications of GQDs, revealing the potential of this material for smart applications such as artificial skin, human-machine interfaces, and health monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Graphene
Strain
Sensor
Electron tunneling

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: S.W. acknowledges the support of a Griffith Publication Assistance Scholarship (PAS). Q.L. wishes to thank the support from Australian Research Council (Nos. DP160104089 , IH 180100002 , and DP 200101105 ). D.C. is grateful for the support of a Griffith University New Researcher Grant. The authors are grateful for the support of centre of Microscopy and Microanalysis (CMM) at the University of Queensland for acquiring SEM and TEM images. The authors also thank the Queensland Node of Australian National Fabrication Facility (ANFF) for their assistance in fabrication of photomasks.

S.W. acknowledges the support of a Griffith Publication Assistance Scholarship (PAS). Q.L. wishes to thank the support from Australian Research Council (Nos. DP160104089, IH 180100002, and DP 200101105). D.C. is grateful for the support of a Griffith University New Researcher Grant. The authors are grateful for the support of centre of Microscopy and Microanalysis (CMM) at the University of Queensland for acquiring SEM and TEM images. The authors also thank the Queensland Node of Australian National Fabrication Facility (ANFF) for their assistance in fabrication of photomasks.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the as-fabricated strain sensor based on electron tunneling of GQDs: flexible interdigitated gold electrodes (thickness ∼30 nm, gaps between each individual pair of interdigitated fet ∼10 µm) were made by lithography on PDMS substrates (thickness ∼300 µm) as shown by the photo in the lower right corner; GQDs solutions were dropcast on the electrodes which filled up the gaps between interdigitated foot; a top thin layer PDMS (thickness∼2 µm) was applied to seal the active part of the device (i.e. interdigitated area) which prevents environmental influences (e.g. humidity, contamination) meanwhile provides localized displacement to GQDs, leaving the two contacts at each end exposed for wiring. The top cartoon shows the zoom-in view of an individual pair of digitated feet of the device, which also depicts the tunneling mechanism of the electrons hopping between adjacent GQDs to form conduction paths.

Synthesized GQDs used for strain sensors in this study: top scheme shows a route combining bottom-up (hydrolysis of citric acid (CA) into carbon sheets (CSs)) and top-down (hydrothermal cutting CSs into GQDs) steps for the synthesis of GQDs. (a) HRTEM image of a quasi-rectangular GQD showing a clear fringe pattern which is a sign of high quality in crystallization; (b) AFM images showing individual GQDs (white dots) scattered on mica substrate; (c) Height profile along the red-dotted line marker in (b) revealing the thickness of two dots on the trace; (d) Height distribution obtained from more than 300 dots giving an average dot thickness around 2.4 nm, suggesting most of the GQDs are multilayered.

Performance of strain sensors: (a–e) test results for a strain sensor fabricated using a GQD solution with a concentration C GQD = 0.4 mg mL–1. (a) I–V curve at undeformed state (i.e. ε = 0) showing nonlinearity which is a signature of electron tunneling. (b) The piezoresistive behavior of the sensor I–V curves shows a gradual decrease in slope with a continuous increase of strain. (c) The correlation between the change rate of tunneling resistance (R–R 0/R 0) and strain showing two linear ranges (i.e. L1 and L2, both are displayed as the independent graph in (d, e), respectively); (f) the correlation between R–R 0/R 0 and strain for a sensor fabricated with another GQD solution (C GQD = 1 mg mL–1) showing piezoresistive behavior maintained over a wider range of strain (over 50%) and three linear ranges suggesting the tunability of the working range of the strain sensors by the concentration of GQDs. All tests were carried out under tension configuration as shown by the schematic on the left panel. Images under the schematic are the photos of a sensor (C GQD = 1 mg mL–1) taken at different strain values as listed beside.

Tunneling and destruction mechanism behind the piezo-resistive behavior of the strain sensors developed in this study: Cartons on the left panel depict the tunneling and destruction mechanism- at the under-formed state (ε 0 = 0), electrons hopping among GQDs forming conduction paths (N 0) between the gold electrodes when the device is deformed by a certain strain (ε > 0) which causes not just the increase of tunneling distance (d > d 0) but also the decrease in the number of conduction paths (N < N 0), both raise the tunneling resistance (R > R 0). d 0 is determined by the π–π stacking and hydrogen bonds among GQDs as shown in the zoom-in view by cartons on the top of the panel; (a) and (b) are the fitting results of the experimental data and the theoretical model for both sensors fabricated with 0.4 mg ml–1 (a) and 1 mg ml–1 (b) of GQD solutions. The tunneling and destruction mechanism sustained over a large strain value of 50% for the sensor in (b).

head.ce:title: Chemically derived graphene quantum dots for high-strain sensing

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Queensland Miro- and Nanotechnology Centre, Griffith University, Nathan Campus, Brisbane QLD 4111, Australia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) refer to graphene fragments with a lateral dimension typically less than 100 nm, which possess unique electrical and optical properties due to the quantum confinement effect. In this study, we demonstrate that chemically derived graphene quantum dots show great potential for making highly stretchable and cost-effective strain sensors via an electron tunneling mechanism. Stretchable strain sensors are critical devices for the field of flexible or wearable electronics which are expected to maintain function up to high strain values (> 30%). However, strain sensors based on conventional materials (i.e. metal or semiconductors) or metal nanoparticles (e.g. gold or silver nanoparticles) only work within a small range of strain (i.e. the former have a working range < 1% and the latter < 3%). In this study, by simply dropcasting solution-processed GQDs between the interdigitated electrodes on polydimethylsiloxane, we obtained devices that can function in the range from 0.06% to over 50% tensile strain with both the sensitivity and working range conveniently adjustable by the concentration of GQDs applied. This study provides a new concept for practical applications of GQDs, revealing the potential of this material for smart applications such as artificial skin, human-machine interfaces, and health monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fragments or nanodiscs of graphene with lateral sizes typically less than 100 nm [1–7]. GQDs are a rising member in the family of graphene derivatives owing to their unique optical, electrical, and magnetic properties arising from the quantum confinement effect and semiconductor nature. They offer profound prospects in various applications including single-electron transistors [8–10], spintronics [11–14], quantum memory [15], etc., though the majority of the GQDs applications have been focused on biomedical and sensing applications, employing the optical properties and their advantage in low-toxicity [16].

A stretchable strain sensor is a fundamental and critical device for the field of flexible electronics. It can be applied to many ‘cutting-edge’ areas including artificial skin (also called electronic skin or ‘e-skin’), health monitoring devices, biomedical devices, human-machine interfaces, and robotics [17–20]. In the field of flexible or wearable electronics, these devices are often required to undergo strain values greater than 30% [21]. Therefore, the conventional strain gauges based on metal (or alloy) foils and semiconductors (e.g. silicon and germanium) are not suitable for this purpose since they can only measure strains of less than 1%. One approach to increase the sensitivity of metallic material for high strains is by embedding liquid metals (i.e. mercury, gallium, or certain alloys) into the elastomers [21–24]. However, the toxicity concerns, cost issues, and complexity of processing restrict their practical use.

In addition to conventional metals, carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and 2D material graphene, have also been studied for strain sensing purposes. However, pristine CNTs or graphene do not provide sensitivity for high strain. For example, graphene sheets prepared by either mechanical exfoliation of graphite or chemical vapor deposition (CVD) have very limited performance for strain sensing in terms of both sensitivity and stretchability (up to 7%) [25,26]. To improve the properties of CNTs and graphene for high-strain sensing, different geometrical structures have to be engineered including alignment of CNTs [27,28], substrate-induced wavy structures of graphene [29], and different 3D hierarchical structures [30–33] (e.g. graphene foam, graphene woven fabric, graphene cellular monolith), which results in a significant increase in the complexity of the device fabrication.

The strain sensing devices mentioned so far are based on the conventional charge conduction (e.g. transport) mechanism, whereby charges are transported throughout a continuous medium. In parallel, electron tunneling is another important mechanism underpinning the piezoresistivity of some strain-sensing devices including films made from metal nanoparticles [34–36] and nanographene films grown by CVD [37]. Electron tunneling is a quantum mechanical effect, which allows the electrons to overcome energy barriers [38]. It enables electrons to hop among conductors which are separated by dielectric gaps, thereby sustaining a current through a disconnected conducting medium.

In practice, the piezo-resistivity enabled by electron tunneling among nanoparticles is dominated by two factors: the change of tunneling distance (d) and the evolution of a number of conducting paths (N) upon modulation of the strain (ε), which could be described by a tunneling and destruction model [37,39] as represented by Eqs. (1) and (2). Briefly, this model reveals that with the increase of strain, the number of conduction paths decreases exponentially, and the tunneling distance increases. Both can be translated into the rise of tunneling resistance.
(1)
N =
N 0
exp
[ −
( α
ε
+ β
ε
2
+ γ
ε
3
+ λ
ε
4
)
]
(2)
ln
( R /
R 0
)
= ln
( 1 +
ε
)
+ α
ε
+ β
ε
2
+ γ
ε
3
+ λ
ε
4

A remarkable feature of the existing tunneling devices is the excellent sensitivity (gage factor up to 700 reported), however, the downside in general is that they can merely work in an extremely short range of strain and the maximum strain value is often lower than 3%. Therefore, the realization of an easy-to-fabricate and cost-effective solution for sensing of high strain (> 30%) as required for flexible electronics is still an ongoing research topic.

Herewith, we report for the first time a highly stretchable and highly tunable strain sensor fabricated by a solution process and enabled by electron tunneling among the GQDs. The GQDs were dropcast between interdigitated gold electrodes (Fig. 1 ) deposited on a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) substrate. These sensors are responsive over a wider range of strain from 0.06% to over 50%, superior to other existing tunneling systems such as metal nanoparticles [34–36] and nanographene [37] (< 3%). Moreover, both the sensitivity (i.e. gage factors) and working range can be tuned by varying the concentration of the deposited GQDs. The behavior of these strain sensors is explained by a tunneling and destruction model, which suggests that the short and conveniently adjustable tunneling distance among GQDs is responsible for their excellent stretchability, as illustrated in Fig. 1. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study on adopting GQDs for strain-sensing purpose.

The GQDs used for this research were synthesized by a green route, integrating both bottom-up and top-down steps (schematic in Fig. 2 ). Carbon sheet structures (CSs) similar to graphite oxide (GO) were firstly synthesized via a bottom-up step through the thermal decomposition of citric acid under 270 °C for a duration of 20 min. The prepared CSs contain crystallites that scatter among the basal planes and could be released through a hydrothermal process at 200 °C. Upon the completion of the whole process, two fractions of samples are obtained, one larger and one smaller than the pore size of a 10 kDa dialysis membrane. Here we employed the larger than 10 kDa GQDs fraction since it contains particles of sizes generally larger than 10 nm, which possess a higher degree of crystallization. Their crystal properties were indicated by their clear crystal fringe structures and sharp edges as shown in Fig. 2(a) measured by HRTEM, and the electron diffraction pattern shown in Fig. S2 (more HRTEM images are available in Supporting Information). The average thickness of the GQDs measured by atomic force microscopy (AFM) is around 2.4 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(b, c). Such a thickness suggests that a considerable fraction of the GQDs are multilayered ones. More specifically, 34% of the particles have a thickness of less than 1.4 nm (i.e. 1–3 layers of carbon atoms) and 56% have a thickness of less than 2.1 nm (i.e. 1–5 layers of carbon atoms), as displayed in Fig. 2(d). In contrast to the parent CSs, the synthesized GQDs are characterized by a lower level of oxygen content as revealed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S3) and the Raman spectra (Fig. S4) shows a prominent G peak (representing sp2 carbon) that is higher than the D peak (representing defective carbon). Both XPS and Raman are consistent with the TEM characterization and confirm the high quality of crystallization of the GQDs.

For the fabrication of the strain sensors, interdigitated gold electrodes (Fig. 1) with a 10 µm gap were made by a photolithography lift-off process on PDMS substrates (average thickness ∼300 µm). 200 µL of GQDs with two different concentrations of 0.4 and 1 mg mL–1 were dropcast on the electrodes. A thin top layer of PDMS (20 µm) was cast to seal the GQDs from environmental contamination and humidity but leaving the two contacts at each end exposed for wiring making the fabrication process simple and scalable.

Fig. 3 (a) shows the I–V curve of a fabricated strain sensor without any deformation (i.e. ε = 0). Different from conventional I–V curves observed with materials such as metal wires, the I–V curve shows a nonlinearity which is a signature for the tunneling feature of the electronic conduction known as percolation [40].

The performance of the strain sensors was measured under tension with a continuous increase of deformation as depicted by the left panel in Fig. 3. Fig. 3(b) exhibits the typical piezo-resistive behavior of a strain sensor dropcast from 200 µL of a GQDs solution with a concentration of 0.4 mg mL–1: the resistance of the strain sensor, as indicated by the slopes of the linear parts of the I–V curves, gradually decreased with the continuous increase of strain. The change of resistance induced by the deformation could be directly visualized by plotting the change rate of resistance (i.e. R–R 0/R 0) against strain (Fig. 3(c)). We notice two linear ranges on the graph, namely, 0.26%–2.37% and 6.07%–14.51%, both are independently displayed in Fig. 3(d, e), respectively. The sensitivity of a strain sensor (or gage) is measured by a parameter called gage factor (GF) which is defined as the ratio between the change rate of the tunneling resistance and the applied strain (i.e. GF = (R–R 0)/εR 0). As shown in Fig. 3(d, e), the GF of the device in the low strain end is around 3.5, which is comparable to commercial grade metal gauges [21]. However, the GF in the higher strain range is approximately 1.

Moreover, Fig. 3(f) exhibits the correlation between R–R 0/R 0 and strain for a sensor fabricated with a concentration of GQDs ∼1 mg mL–1. The results indicate that the tunneling resistance rises gradually with the increase of strain, similar to the sensor in Fig. 3(c), however, the response continued up to strain values over 50%. In this case, there are three linear ranges presented, 0%–2.24%, 2.53%–14.01%, and 15.19%–55.83%, as shown in Fig. 3(d). These results suggest that the working range of the device can be tuned by the concentration of the GQDs. More about the performance of strain sensors in this study is available in Figs. S5–S7.

To understand the excellent stretch ability and tunability of our sensors, we fitted our experimental data with the aforementioned tunneling and destruction model (detail of the fitting is described in supporting information). This model Eqs. (1) and ((2)) correlates the change of tunneling resistance (R) to the change of both tunneling distance (d) and a number of conduction paths (N). As shown by the cartoons in the left panel of Fig. 4 , increasing device deformation not just widens the tunneling distance (d > d 0) but also decreases the number of conduction paths (N < N 0) formed among GQDs. Both changes in d and N raise R. Fig. 4(a, b) shows a good agreement between our experimental data and the tunneling and destruction model. One essential parameter that can be derived from this model is the initial tunneling distance d 0 , which is related to the parameter α and is proportional to d 0 (i.e. α = Xd 0). The calculated d 0 of the strain sensors is in the range of 0–0.5 nm and it is tunable by changing the concentration of the dispersion of GQDs (see supporting information for the detail of the calculation).

A widely accepted cut-off distance for electron tunneling among nanoparticles is around 10 nm [21]. Moreover, the initial tunneling distance reported in other nanoparticle systems (e.g. ∼3 nm for nanographene) [37] is much larger than the values derived in this study. Hence, such short initial tunneling distances ensure the hopping of electrons between separate GQDs can be maintained over a large range of device deformation (over 50%). We attribute the short initial tunneling distance to the ubiquitous attractions among GQDs particles including the formation of hydrogen bonds (bond length = 0.2–0.4 nm) [41] among oxygenated functional groups and π–π stacking (bond length = 0.3–0.4 nm) [42,43] between aromatic structures whose attraction ranges are consistent with the initial tunneling distances we derived (0–0.5 nm). In contrast, surfactants or organic ligands are usually adopted in the synthesis of colloidal metal particles [44], these relatively ‘large’ species, eventually form much thicker dielectric gaps, namely large initial tunneling distances, among metal particles. As a result, the electron hopping can only last in much shorter strain ranges (typically < 3%) before the extinction of conduction paths.

In conclusion, we demonstrated for the first time that chemically derived GQDs have great potential for developing highly stretchable, easily tunable, and cost-effective strain sensors, which are important devices for smart applications including artificial skin, human-machine interfaces, and health monitoring. Without device optimization, the strain sensors developed by a simple dropcast procedure show sensitivity to strain values over 50% and the sensitivity could be conveniently tuned by varying the concentration of dispersions of GQDs. The behaviors of our devices fit well with a tunneling and destruction model. The model fitting suggests that the initial tunneling distances of the sensors depend on the concentration of GQDs and they are much shorter (0–0.5 nm) than those in existing tunneling systems (1–10 nm) typically employing gold nanoparticles, nanographene films derived from CVD and CNTs. We consider that the tunable short initial tunneling distance may be the chief reason for the excellent stretchability and tunable sensitivity of the sensors, which also invites further fundamental studies on the detailed mechanisms of electron tunneling between GQDs. In particular, the geometry of the GQDs is typically anisotropic, and how would this factor plays a role in electron tunneling and how to utilize or circumvent this effect is yet to be further investigated. This study unveils the potential of applying solution-processed GQDs in nanoelectronics and smart applications including artificial skin, human-machine interfaces, and health monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: S.W. acknowledges the support of a Griffith Publication Assistance Scholarship (PAS). Q.L. wishes to thank the support from Australian Research Council (Nos. DP160104089, IH 180100002, and DP 200101105). D.C. is grateful for the support of a Griffith University New Researcher Grant. The authors are grateful for the support of centre of Microscopy and Microanalysis (CMM) at the University of Queensland for acquiring SEM and TEM images. The authors also thank the Queensland Node of Australian National Fabrication Facility (ANFF) for their assistance in fabrication of photomasks.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jmst.2022.08.041.
